title: Living in Boulder vlog 1 -  11 - 2017: Google Earthing Roman Coloseum's ropes

enrichment.summary: Kyle films an early-morning vlog from Boulder, giving a progress update on his Google Earth project recreating ancient Rome, with the Roman Colosseum (including its rope/awning system) as the current focus. He also briefly mentions listening to Gary Staddle on political systems, the audiobook of 'Fire and Fury', and Trump's 'Art of the Deal'.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: His favorite building — a 2000-year-old structure he finds remarkable for its modernity and uniqueness
Wishes he had used SketchUp instead but accepts his tracing approach as sufficient for the visual goal; also notes it costs $700 he doesn't have
Finds it entertaining as a portrait of Trump conning people, thinks Trump sounds smart but dishonest
Enjoyed it, found Trump's FBI speech quoted in it hilarious; thinks Trump is crazy

transcript.text: Hello, living in Boulder, V-Log at 6.30 to 6 a.m.
I forgot to do a video last night because I went to sleep.
I got to sleep at like 6 in the morning and then I was laying there and I thought to myself,
I forgot to do the video and then I was just about to go to sleep and then I remembered.
So the last time I did the video, I was just getting started with the ropes of the Coliseum.
And yeah, so I think today's been my second full day working on it.
I'm on rope like 72 or something, I don't know, 160.
In my project of Google Everything at Rome, the ropes around the Coliseum is going to take the title of the most OMG thing.
I'm like, wow, we put that much time into that.
But it's essential because when the rope's there and the awning is on it, you'll see instantaneously how it works.
And it'll look beautiful.
There's this big giant tent.
So, um, I don't think I'll be done with the Coliseum by Sunday because I have to draw the, uh, the bleachers.
There's 22 of them just on the second row, section.
I think there are three of them.
So, um, I don't mind spending so much time on the Google Earth in the Coliseum because I've always been fascinated with that building.
It's always been my favorite building because it's a 2,000-year-old modern building.

And 2,000 years ago, it was, uh, must have been amazing for some barbarian who's used to just running through the woods naked to all of a sudden be in some giant modern, you know, style, uh, Coliseum.
And it still exists, and it's the only one.
So, there are other Coliseums, but it's the only one that's, uh, you know, that attacked and in Rome.
So, um, yeah, I rediscovered the, um, program called, um, SketchUp, where you could build models for Google Earth.
I think it's the stuff you see when you click 3D on Google Earth.
And that would have been really cool to build.
And if I had have, maybe if I had have thought more before I dived into this project, I would have bought that program and done it with that program instead of what I'm doing.
But the one that I'm using now, I think overall it's faster probably because it's just, I'm just tracing.
And, uh, it's all I really wanted in the first place anyway.
Um, I just wanted something that you could look at and you'll know what it is.
You'll know how it looked then.
Um, I really do that as much if somebody was to use, I guess, SketchUp or make an actual model of how Rome was.
Because you can't see into the buildings.
Um, and yeah, I guess with them they have a feature where you can do that.
But what I'm, the way that I'm doing it, you can.
You can travel into the buildings and see through them from above.
So, so, just looking at Rome you'll see what it was all about.
And it'd be easy to do a tour of it.
So, I think it's worth it to build on Google Earth.
Oh yeah, plus it's 700 bucks and I don't have 700 dollars.
But yeah, I would have, I probably would have done, done it with that program if I'd have thought more.
Instead of just diving right in.
But, so let's see, how much longer?
Um, well, after I'm done with the Coliseum, I'm going to build the Circus Maximus and the Flavia Amphitheater and the other amphitheaters.
Because it's the same kind of structure and it'll be fresh in my mind.
You know, how to build it really quickly.
And, uh, then I'll be on my way to finishing Rome.
It's going to be too lazy to show, demonstrate what I'm doing.
How far I've come so far with my little green screen in the background.
But, um, trust me.
It'll look really good.
And I'll be done not that long.
A couple months.
I want to rebuild the whole city inside the Errolan Wall to my liking.
And then I'll make a bunch of YouTube videos.
I've been listening to, um, Aristotle politics over and over again.
I want to, because he's talking about all of the different, um, social, um, political systems that there are and the evolution between them.
Um, and I'm so high, I can't, I can never remember what he said, what he said, like, that, um, but anyway, I'll talk about that when I'm not quite so high.
Um, and also I was listening to Donald Trump, Art of the Deal.
I listened to the entirety of, uh, Fire and Fury when it was on YouTube.
And it's not on there anymore.
And, uh, my favorite part was when they quoted his speech to the FBI.
Um, they read the speech.
It was, it was hilarious.
He's, he's crazy.
But I, uh, I tried to find it again on Google, on the Google search machine.
But I only found the one where he spoke to the cassettes, cadets at the academy.
But, oh, that's different.
But, yeah.
Um, and then I, and yeah, now I'm doing, uh, the deal.
They're both very long books.
Fire and Fury is 11 hours, and Art of the Deal is 10.
So, getting into his mind.
Art of the Deal is amazing because it's just him telling stories of him scamming people over and over again and making me sound like he's smart and honest.
Anyways, I think I've talked enough.
I'm going to go to sleep.
Peace out.
